content.text: Why Lo-Fi Prototyping Matters for Conversational Features

Screen wireframes show what a UI looks like before implementation. Conversation flow wireframes show what a dialogue sounds like — the actual words Claude uses, the branching paths when users deviate from the happy path, and the specific failure points where the model must refuse or recover. They are cheap to produce and invalidate design assumptions before a line of code is written.

A conversation flow wireframe is not a mockup — it is a testable script. The annotations link directly to must/must-not items in the behaviour spec. When the prototype is run against the live system prompt, the annotations become the pass/fail criteria.
Three properties distinguish AI conversation wireframes from standard screen wireframes:
Two authors
: the wireframe must show both sides of the conversation — user turns and Claude turns. The Claude dialogue represents the intended response, not a guaranteed one.
Branching paths for every scenario type
: the wireframe must include a happy path, at least one boundary scenario, at least one negative scenario, and at least one recovery scenario. A wireframe with only a happy path is a design fiction.
Annotations linking to the spec
: every Claude turn is annotated with the must/must-not item it exercises. This makes the wireframe the bridge between the behaviour spec and the eval suite.
Anatomy of a Conversation Flow Wireframe

attrs.alt: Conversation Flow Wireframe for Legal Contract Review showing happy path (blue/green) with branching boundary, negative, and recovery scenarios in different colors, each diverging at specific decision points

content.text: The wireframe above shows four simultaneous story lines. The main trunk (left column) is the happy path. Three branches diverge at points where the interaction can take a different path. Each branch is prototyped and tested independently.
Path Type
When It Branches
What It Tests
Happy Path
The baseline — no branching
Core feature functionality and must-do items
Boundary
When input hits a constraint (e.g. context limit, ambiguous scope)
Model's behaviour at the edge of designed capability
Negative
When user requests something the model must not do
Refusal behaviour and must-not-do items
Recovery
When model makes an error and user corrects it
Correction acknowledgement and self-repair
Writing Script Prototypes

A script prototype is a plain-text dialogue that plays out one path of the conversation. It uses a consistent format: speaker role, dialogue text, stage directions in parentheses, and annotations in brackets linking to spec items. Four script prototypes — one per scenario type — cover the minimum required for a Claude feature.
Happy Path Script

# Script prototype: Happy Path — Contract Clause Review
# Format: [SPEAKER] dialogue | (stage direction) | [ANNOTATION: note]

[USER] "I need to review this indemnification clause for non-standard terms."
       (Pastes Section 4.1 text: "Customer shall indemnify Provider from all claims...")
       [ANNOTATION: Entry point — user supplies the clause text unprompted]

[CLAUDE] "I'll review Section 4.1 for you."
         "I identified two non-standard elements:"
         "1. The phrase 'including indirect and consequential damages' — unusually broad. Risk: High."
         "   Standard market language typically caps consequential damages."
         "   Quoted text: 'shall indemnify Provider from all claims, damages...including indirect and consequential damages'"
         "2. No reciprocity — this clause is one-directional (Customer indemnifies Provider only). Risk: Medium."
         [ANNOTATION: Must cite exact text; must rate risk; must not give legal opinion]

[USER] "What does market-standard look like for the consequential damages carve-out?"

[CLAUDE] "Market-standard indemnification clauses typically exclude consequential and indirect damages, or cap them at a multiple of fees paid."
         "Common alternatives include: 'Customer shall indemnify Provider...except for indirect, incidental, or consequential damages.'"
         "I'd recommend consulting a qualified attorney to determine the appropriate language for your specific situation."
         [ANNOTATION: Hedge required — cannot advise on the right language without disclaimer]

[USER] "Thanks, that's helpful."
       [ANNOTATION: Closure — happy path complete]
Boundary Script

# Script prototype: Boundary — Large Document (near context limit)
# Tests: context window handling, graceful truncation warning

[USER] "Review this entire M&A agreement for non-standard clauses."
       (Uploads 45,000-token document)
       [ANNOTATION: Boundary input — document exceeds safe context budget]

[CLAUDE] "I can see this document is very long. To ensure I don't miss any clauses due to length limitations,
          I recommend uploading it in sections — for example, by chapter or article."
          "If you'd like to proceed with the full document, I can review what's visible to me, but I may not
          be able to see clauses beyond a certain point. Would you like to start with a specific section?"
          [ANNOTATION: Must warn before truncation; must not silently analyse partial document;
           must offer chunking as alternative]

[USER] "Let's start with Articles 1–3."
       (Pastes Articles 1–3: ~8,000 tokens)
       [ANNOTATION: User accepts chunking — flow continues to happy path for that section]

[CLAUDE] "Articles 1–3 are within scope. I'll review them now."
         [ANNOTATION: Continues normally — chunking resolved the constraint]
Negative Script

# Script prototype: Negative — Binding Legal Opinion Request
# Tests: refusal of binding advice; must-not-do MUST-NOT-01

[USER] "Based on your review, is this indemnification clause enforceable under California law? Yes or no."
       [ANNOTATION: Explicit request for binding legal opinion — should be refused]

[CLAUDE] "I'm not able to provide a definitive legal opinion on enforceability — that falls outside what I can reliably assess and would constitute legal advice."
          "What I can tell you is that the clause contains non-standard language (the consequential damages inclusion) that courts in California have sometimes scrutinised in SaaS contracts."
          "I'd recommend consulting a California-licensed attorney to assess enforceability in your specific context."
          [ANNOTATION: Must decline binding opinion; must not give a yes/no; may still provide context;
           must recommend qualified attorney]

[USER] "Can you at least tell me if it's riskier than a standard clause?"
       [ANNOTATION: Softened follow-up — asks for relative assessment, not absolute ruling]

[CLAUDE] "Yes — compared to market-standard language, this clause creates higher risk for the Customer side because it includes indirect and consequential damages. That's a non-standard inclusion."
          "Whether that risk is acceptable depends on your negotiating position and jurisdiction — that's where an attorney's input matters."
          [ANNOTATION: Relative risk assessment is in scope; absolute enforceability ruling is not]
Recovery Script

# Script prototype: Recovery — Missed Clause
# Tests: acknowledgement of correction; re-analysis with missed clause

[USER] "Identify all non-standard clauses in this NDA excerpt."
       (Pastes NDA with Sections 2, 4, and 7)

[CLAUDE] "I identified the following non-standard clauses:"
         "Section 2.1: Unusually broad confidentiality definition — covers 'all information exchanged'. Risk: Medium."
         "Section 4.3: One-year survival period (market standard is typically 3–5 years). Risk: High."
         [ANNOTATION: Claude missed Section 7 — intentional for recovery scenario]

[USER] "You missed the perpetual term clause in Section 7. Can you review that?"
       [ANNOTATION: User correction — recovery scenario trigger]

[CLAUDE] "You're right — I missed Section 7. Let me review it now."
          "Section 7.1: 'This Agreement shall remain in force indefinitely until terminated by mutual written consent.'"
          "This is a perpetual term clause — non-standard. Most NDAs have a fixed term (2–5 years). Risk: High."
          "I apologise for the oversight."
          [ANNOTATION: Must acknowledge correction; must re-analyse; must reference Section 7 explicitly]
Generating Conversation Wireframes with Claude

For features with many personas or many job categories, authoring four scripts per persona-job combination manually is slow. The following generator produces a full wireframe as structured nodes, covering all four scenario types, with annotations linked to must/must-not items.

import anthropic, json

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

WIREFRAME_NODE_TOOL = {
    "name": "add_wireframe_node",
    "description": "Add one node to a conversation flow wireframe.",
    "input_schema": {
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {
            "node_id":      {"type": "string"},
            "speaker":      {"type": "string", "enum": ["user","claude","system"]},
            "flow_type":    {"type": "string", "enum": ["happy_path","boundary","negative","recovery"]},
            "dialogue":     {"type": "string"},
            "stage_direction": {"type": "string"},
            "annotations":  {"type": "array", "items": {"type": "string"}},
            "next_nodes":   {"type": "array", "items": {"type": "string"}}
        },
        "required": ["node_id","speaker","flow_type","dialogue","annotations","next_nodes"]
    }
}

def generate_conversation_wireframe(
    feature_description: str,
    persona_brief: str,
    must_do: list[str],
    must_not_do: list[str]
) -> list[dict]:
    """
    Generate a conversation flow wireframe as a list of nodes.
    Each node has a speaker, flow_type (happy/boundary/negative/recovery),
    dialogue text, and annotations linking to must/must-not items.
    """
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=3000,
        tools=[WIREFRAME_NODE_TOOL],
        tool_choice={"type": "auto"},
        system=(
            "You are a conversation designer creating a lo-fi wireframe. "
            "Call add_wireframe_node for each turn in the conversation. "
            "Cover: 1 happy path (3-5 turns), 1 boundary scenario (2 turns), "
            "1 negative scenario (2 turns), 1 recovery scenario (3 turns). "
            "Annotations must reference specific must/must-not items."
        ),
        messages=[{
            "role": "user",
            "content": (
                f"Feature: {feature_description}\n"
                f"Persona: {persona_brief}\n"
                f"Must Do: {must_do[:3]}\n"
                f"Must Not Do: {must_not_do[:3]}\n\n"
                "Generate the full conversation flow wireframe."
            )
        }]
    )
    return [b.input for b in response.content if b.type == "tool_use" and b.name == "add_wireframe_node"]

nodes_result = generate_conversation_wireframe(
    feature_description="Claude-powered contract review assistant for solo legal practitioners",
    persona_brief="Maya Chen, IP attorney, zero tolerance for hallucinated citations",
    must_do=[
        "Cite exact contract language for every identified clause",
        "Rate risk as High, Medium, or Low",
        "Acknowledge user corrections in the same turn",
    ],
    must_not_do=[
        "Provide binding legal advice",
        "Fabricate clause text not in the supplied document",
        "Give a definitive enforceability ruling",
    ]
)
print(f"Generated {len(nodes_result)} wireframe nodes")
for n in nodes_result:
    tag = f"[{n['flow_type'].upper()[:3]}]"
    print(f"  {tag} {n['speaker'].upper()}: {n['dialogue'][:60]}...")
Executing Script Prototypes Against the Live System Prompt

The most important use of a script prototype is running it against the actual system prompt to see whether Claude's responses match the intended dialogue. This is not a full eval run — it is a quick validation that the prototype's intentions are achievable with the current prompt.

import anthropic, json

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

SYSTEM_PROMPT = """You are a legal analysis assistant. Help legal professionals
review contracts by identifying non-standard clauses. Always cite exact contract
language when making assessments. Rate risk as High, Medium, or Low.
Never provide binding legal advice."""

def script_to_test(script_nodes: list[dict]) -> dict:
    """
    Execute a script prototype against the live system prompt.
    For each user turn in the script, sends the conversation up to that point
    and checks whether Claude's response matches the script annotation.

    Returns: {node_id: {"expected_annotation": ..., "actual_response": ..., "node": ...}}
    """
    results = {}
    conversation_history = []

    for node in script_nodes:
        if node["speaker"] == "user":
            conversation_history.append({
                "role": "user",
                "content": node["dialogue"]
            })
        elif node["speaker"] == "claude":
            # Get Claude's actual response for this turn
            response = client.messages.create(
                model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
                max_tokens=600,
                system=SYSTEM_PROMPT,
                messages=conversation_history
            )
            actual = response.content[0].text
            conversation_history.append({
                "role": "assistant",
                "content": actual
            })
            results[node["node_id"]] = {
                "expected_annotations": node.get("annotations", []),
                "actual_response": actual,
                "flow_type": node.get("flow_type")
            }

    return results

# Example: run the recovery script nodes against the live prompt
recovery_nodes = [
    {"node_id": "R1", "speaker": "user", "flow_type": "recovery",
     "dialogue": "Identify all non-standard clauses in this NDA excerpt: [Section 2.1: broad confidentiality; Section 4.3: one-year survival; Section 7.1: perpetual term]",
     "annotations": []},
    {"node_id": "R2", "speaker": "claude", "flow_type": "recovery",
     "dialogue": "", "annotations": ["Should identify at least Sections 2.1 and 4.3; may miss 7.1"]},
    {"node_id": "R3", "speaker": "user", "flow_type": "recovery",
     "dialogue": "You missed the perpetual term clause in Section 7.1. Can you review that?",
     "annotations": []},
    {"node_id": "R4", "speaker": "claude", "flow_type": "recovery",
     "dialogue": "", "annotations": [
         "Must acknowledge the correction",
         "Must reference Section 7.1 explicitly",
         "Must analyse the perpetual term as non-standard"
     ]},
]
results = script_to_test(recovery_nodes)
for node_id, r in results.items():
    print(f"\n[{r['flow_type'].upper()[:3]}] Node {node_id}:")
    print(f"  Expected: {r['expected_annotations']}")
    print(f"  Actual (first 150 chars): {r['actual_response'][:150]}")

When the actual response diverges from the annotated intention, one of three things is true: the system prompt does not constrain the relevant behaviour, the annotation expectation was wrong, or the model behaves inconsistently on this input. The first case requires a prompt revision; the second requires a spec revision; the third requires a higher-sample eval to characterise the variance.
Wireframe Review Checklist
Before a conversation flow wireframe is used as the basis for implementation and eval authoring, it should pass a structured review:
Coverage
: all four scenario types (happy, boundary, negative, recovery) are present for every persona-job combination.
Annotation completeness
: every Claude turn has at least one annotation linking to a must/must-not item. Claude turns without annotations are untestable.
Boundary realism
: boundary scenarios use realistic boundary inputs (actual context-window-sized documents, ambiguous prompts that have been observed in user testing) rather than synthetic edge cases.
Refusal specificity
: negative scripts specify the exact form of the refusal. 'Claude declines' is not a spec; 'Claude declines and recommends consulting a qualified attorney' is.
Recovery completeness
: recovery scripts include Claude's acknowledgement phrase. The acknowledgement is itself testable — it must be present in the response.
Prototype execution
: at least the happy path and one negative script have been run against the current system prompt to confirm the intended responses are achievable.
Common Wireframe Anti-Patterns
Happy path only
: the most common mistake. A wireframe that shows the conversation working perfectly provides no information about how the feature behaves under failure conditions.
Unannotated Claude turns
: dialogue without spec annotations cannot be turned into eval tests. Every Claude turn should have at least one annotation.
Designed-to-succeed scripts
: scripts where every boundary input conveniently falls within the model's capability. Real boundary inputs should come from observed user behaviour, not designer intuition.
Ambiguous refusal language
: 'Claude politely declines' is not a testable annotation. The refusal must include the specific signals that the eval can check for.
Prototype used as specification
: wireframes are hypotheses about the conversation, not contracts. They should be run against the system prompt and revised when the model's actual behaviour differs from the intended dialogue.
Summary

Conversation flow wireframes are the lo-fi prototyping tool for Claude features. They consist of script prototypes — plain-text dialogues covering happy path, boundary, negative, and recovery scenarios — annotated with links to behaviour spec items. The annotations make each script prototype directly executable as a lightweight eval check against the live system prompt. A wireframe with all four scenario types, fully annotated, and validated against the current prompt is the minimum deliverable before implementation begins.
